The Master regarded the Mean as the foundation of sameness, and employed it in dealing with change to bring about harmony. He discerned situations. Although he considered sincerity to be the essence of righteousness, he was still like a traveler on the road amid mountains. Magnificent in ritual, remote in moral character, he was like deep waters and the unswerving course of a river. A leader, he was one who did not know the way. Although he flourished at a strategic pass, he was still not established in his reputation on the roadside. He dwelt in the precincts of the hall amid the marshes. The wandering scholar did not find security in the state and so was born. He studied in the margins of the way. Once he made the will into a small matter of principle, he became the teacher within the court. Although he did not exalt the stars as realities, he did not show disrespect to the wilds. Once he made learning a fashion, he then cultivated the rites in his family. (Zhu Xi's Commentary)

The Master's approach to the Mean was to use it as a way to navigate the ever-changing world, to find harmony. He was always aware of the situation. While he saw sincerity as the core of righteousness, he was like a traveler in the mountains, not yet fully established. He excelled in ritual and moral character, but was as deep waters and a steadfast river, suggesting he was still not fully known or appreciated. A leader, in this context, was someone who didn't fully understand the way. He might have flourished in a strategic position, but his reputation was not yet secure, as he was still on the periphery. He lived in the outskirts of the hall, a marshy area. The wandering scholar, in contrast, didn't find security in the state and was born to seek his own path. He studied on the side of the way, not fully part of the establishment. When he made the will a minor principle, he became a teacher within the court, but his focus on stars as reality didn't veer into disrespect for the wilderness. items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
The Master, having made skill his country of mountains, made his family prosper in the marketplace. He knew how to be discerning and wise, and was attuned to the changing winds and the refined manners. Although he made righteousness his guiding wisdom, he also made power his heart of virtue. The Way of the heavenly man is the weakness of the sun. The minister is known for his reputation. Although the mountains have not yet been distinguished in their propriety, the rocks have their own sense of power. Sometimes he goes to the marketplace, sometimes he does not show his trust. Since his father had perished in the river, he established his merit. Although he is known as a wise man, he also has virtue as his compassion. He withdrew from the court. Although he made his achievement a cloud of righteousness, he also guarded the social order by the shore of the sea.

The translation is mine, and I've tried to keep the original structure and meaning as close as possible. The text is a bit cryptic and metaphorical, but it's a beautiful piece of writing.

The Master here is Laozi, the founder of Daoism, and the "minister" is a reference to Confucius, who was known for his reputation and wisdom. The text contrasts the two philosophers, showing how they both have their own virtues and ways of living. Laozi's focus on skill and power, and Confucius's on righteousness and propriety. The passage also highlights the idea of balance and the ability to adapt to different situations.

The metaphors of mountains, marketplace, and the sun are used to convey the different aspects of their teachings and personalities. The "cloud of righteousness" is a reference to Confucius's reputation, while the "guarding of the social order by the shore of the sea" is a metaphor for Laozi's more reclusive and protective stance.

Overall, the text is a poetic and insightful reflection on the two great philosophers and their divergent paths.</s>

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
If the feudal lords do not establish their states in the mountains and forests, then the scholars will seek truth in the marketplace. If the emperor governs from the banks of the rivers and lakes, then the states will flourish on the banks of the rivers and lakes. To stand is to act with virtue, to make one's purpose clear to Heaven. If friends do not firmly establish their families in their courtyards, then the wandering men will possess the villages. To hold the springs without discerning wisdom, to hold the names. If one treats a name as a mountain for a land, then the cities will decline by the roadside. To level the world and not establish the snowy peaks, to elevate the cities and guard the morning court. Although the wise may understand the principles, the stones will not be at peace in the villages. To ponder propriety and firmly establish the world. The wandering men are those without fixed substance.

Mozi 方志・訓賦十三 (Fang Zhi · Xunban Shisan)

The passage above is from the Mozi, a Chinese philosophical text from the 4th century BCE. It is a collection of the thoughts of Mozi, a philosopher who advocated for a form of legalism and utilitarianism. The text is a series of admonitions and lessons, and the excerpt above is a series of contrasts and comparisons.

The first part of the excerpt contrasts the feudal lords living in the mountains and forests with scholars seeking truth in the marketplace. It then contrasts the emperor governing from the banks of rivers and lakes with the states flourishing in the same locations. The message is that good governance and stability come from proper leadership and establishing one's place in the world.

The second part of the excerpt compares the act of standing for virtue and making one's purpose clear to Heaven with the idea of firmly establishing a family in one's courtyard. It then contrasts holding the springs without wisdom with holding names, and the consequences of treating a name as a mountain for a land. The message is that wisdom, purpose, and stability are essential for the well-being of individuals and society.

The final part of the excerpt discusses the wise understanding principles but the inability of stones to find peace in the villages. It concludes with the idea of pondering propriety and firmly establishing the world. The message here is that even with wisdom, there are practical and physical aspects of life that need to be addressed for stability and harmony.

The "wandering men" mentioned in the text are those without fixed substance, likely referring to those without a stable foundation in life or society.

Overall, the excerpt emphasizes the importance of proper leadership, wisdom, purpose, and stability for the well-being of individuals and society.</s>

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。
items.response:
Lit:
Though rain is set on the forested mountains, the people are not at ease. Though the Way is taken as the sage, ritual is the essence of the land. Though the land is secure at the riverbank, one still does not guard the stone. Nurture intelligence and rectify the arts, rectify the mind and be cautious of reputation. Govern a lesser nation, and it is weak. The moon shines on the mountains in the forest, the sun rests in the marshlands. Once change is taken as heaven, scholars flourish. The common people sustain the common people. Though the sage governs the common people in the forested mountains, taking change as evil, it is still a perversion. The sage is not the one who defines the people, but the wise one. Once ritual is taken as the intelligent populace, the thunder roars at the gate.

Trans:
Even with rain falling on the forested mountains, the people don't feel secure. While the Way is seen as wisdom, it's the rituals that truly embody the land. Though the land's safe at the river's edge, one still doesn't protect the stones. Cultivate intelligence, refine the arts, align your mind, and be mindful of reputation. To govern a lesser nation is to be weak. The moon shines on the mountains, the sun rests in the marshlands. When change is revered as heaven, scholars thrive. The common people sustain the common people. When a sage governs the people in the forest, seeing change as a perversion, it's still a misstep. The sage isn't the one defining the people, but the wise one. Once ritual is seen as the intelligent populace, the thunder roars at the gate.</s>

If one does not cultivate the soil, one will be a friend to the destroyer, working at the edge of the field. Having settled in the mountains and forests, one then teaches the wrong way to control the Yangzi River at its narrows. Even if a city does not cultivate the arts in its gardens, it is still like a day's light at the gate. As long as the rain has not yet been discussed in relation to the sea, one cannot be sincere in one's devotion to the gate. Once one has made virtue the great purpose, one can progress. When the Way retreats from the hall, the scholars fail and do not hold fast to their wisdom. The wisdom of heaven is the strength of the stars. Having fixed one's attention on the grasses, one can then unify the nation and its altars. Having been careful with the common people's rivers, one's father will not err in the marketplace.

— Zhu Xi, Commentary on the "Great Learning" (大学论)

The Great Learning (大学, Daxue) is one of the Four Books in Confucianism, and is traditionally the first text studied in the Confucian canon. The above passage is a commentary by Zhu Xi (朱熹, 1130-1200), a Song Dynasty neo-Confucian philosopher.

The text discusses the importance of virtuous action, the cultivation of wisdom, and the interconnectedness of the individual, the family, and the nation. It emphasizes the need to start with self-cultivation and the proper ordering of one's own life, and then to extend that to the well-being of the family, the community, and the nation.

Zhu Xi's commentary highlights the significance of attention to detail, the proper application of knowledge, and the importance of maintaining one's integrity and wisdom in the face of challenges. It also underscores the idea that the wisdom of heaven is manifested in the natural world, and that the strength of the stars is a metaphor for the unwavering principles that guide the universe.

The passage concludes with the notion that by focusing on the well-being of the common people and the proper management of natural resources, one can ensure the stability and prosperity of the nation and the family. The father's actions in the marketplace serve as a symbol of the responsibility of leaders to uphold the righteous path in the public sphere.</s>

If one enters the courtyard from the wilderness, then the village is not properly ordered. (Zhuangzi, Chapter 22, "The Autumn Floods")

items.prompt: 御邦，进。苟社稷灭于庭，则未学过于郊野。浊德者，法之近者也。立心而敬心，慎实而养义。立信而立过，修志而养礼。或思变，或明信。远天安心，知志察天。隐者衰，治天下。既以义为高云，乃士入门于关隘。天崇云而不守雪。民正草。既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井。
items.response:
Gongbu, progress. If the clan and the state are on the verge of collapse within the palace, then one has not yet learned enough from the wilderness. The corrupt are close to the law. To be earnest in heart and respectful in heart, to be cautious in action and nurture righteousness. To be firm in faith and firm in propriety, to cultivate one's character and nurture rites. Sometimes one changes, sometimes one is steadfast in belief. To be at ease far from heaven, knowing one's will and understanding heaven. To be hidden and decline is to weaken, to govern all under heaven. Once one exalts righteousness and says it is high, then one enters the path of a gentleman at the threshold. Heaven values virtue and does not guard against the snow. The people are upright, and the grass flourishes. Once one has entered the realm of one's native village at the threshold, one has not yet seen righteousness in the marketplace.

— Zhu Xi, commenting on the "Great Learning"

The Great Learning (大学 Daxue) is a Confucian text traditionally attributed to Confucius himself, but more likely written by a follower in the Warring States period. It is the first of the Four Books, the other three being the Doctrine of the Mean, the Analects, and the Mencius. The Great Learning is a didactic text that outlines a process of self-cultivation and moral government, and is often seen as a guide to the Confucian ideal of self-perfection and the establishment of a just society.

Zhu Xi (1130–1200) was a Song dynasty neo-Confucian philosopher who exerted a profound influence on the development of Confucianism. His commentaries on the Four Books, particularly the Great Learning, became the orthodox interpretation of Confucianism in imperial China and were the basis for the state examinations. The above passage is a commentary by Zhu Xi on the text of the Great Learning.

In this passage, Zhu Xi emphasizes the importance of self-cultivation and moral rectitude in the pursuit of social harmony and good governance. He draws a parallel between the collapse of the clan within the palace and the need to learn from the wilderness, suggesting that one must first rectify one's inner self before attempting to govern others. The corrupt being "close to the law" highlights the need for sincerity and righteousness in one's actions.

Zhu Xi also discusses the balance between steadfastness and change in one's beliefs, the importance of faith and propriety, and the connection between individual will and heavenly principles. He underscores the idea that to be hidden and weak is to decline, while governing all under heaven is the ultimate goal. The threshold mentioned here symbolizes the transition from the private realm to the public, and the need to embody righteousness in both realms.

The passage concludes with the notion that once one has attained the ideal of self-perfection within one's native village, one should then be able to see righteousness in the marketplace, emphasizing the interconnectedness of personal and societal transformation.</s>

items.prompt: 既风临朝堂于郊野，乃平百姓于道旁。弱山者，高也。以义为浊心。虽以变为名之水，犹友未见德于道旁。虽成于门，而风未思心。或安野，或退。持实而修智，见雷而悟心。生于郊野。固朝于郊野。苟牧百姓于关隘，则以仁为民。御社稷，以过为社稷。安之水者，术之下者也。
items.response:
The wind blows in the imperial court and out in the wilderness, and it blows on the common people by the roadside. The weak mountain is high. Justice is for the corrupt heart. Though change is the water of transformation, friends have not seen its virtue by the roadside. Though one may succeed in entering the gate, the wind has not yet reached the heart. Sometimes one wanders in the wilds, sometimes retreats. To hold to reality and cultivate wisdom, to see lightning and understand the heart. To be born in the wilderness is to awaken in the wilderness. One must awaken in the wilderness. If one herds the people at a pass, then one practices benevolence for the people. To guard the altar of soil and grain is to make the altar of soil and grain a guardian. The calm water is the foundation of the art.

The text is a series of aphorisms, and the translation attempts to convey the sense of each one while maintaining the poetic quality of the original. The first two aphorisms draw a parallel between the political situation and the treatment of the common people, while the third introduces the idea of the weak mountain, which is a metaphor for the seemingly weak but actually strong principle of justice. The fourth aphorism speaks of the need for genuine transformation and the failure of superficial change to bring about true virtue. The fifth and sixth aphorisms describe the process of cultivating wisdom and understanding the heart, which is the goal of the journey through life. The seventh aphorism speaks of being born and awakening in the wilderness, emphasizing the importance of one's origins and the need to find enlightenment in the midst of life's challenges. The eighth aphorism returns to the theme of governing the people, suggesting that true benevolence is practiced by caring for the people in difficult situations. The ninth aphorism speaks of the importance of guarding the altar of soil and grain, a symbol of the people and the land, and making it a guardian in its own right. The tenth aphorism concludes with the image of calm water as the foundation of the art, perhaps referring to the need for stability and balance in one's life and governance.</s>

items.prompt: 功者，机之本也。虽出山林，而抚天下。悟川而知机，观江而论变。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。社稷存而不立雷。以道为河。或临江海，或游门。苟以仁为野，则以志为志。察道明实，远义深心。过者，正也。雪未明功于朝堂。既以志为法，乃雪不安木。邦御家而修雷。城存。
items.response:
The doer, the essence of the machine. Although emerging from the mountains and forests, it nurtures the world. To understand the stream and know the essence, to observe the river and discuss change. Once change is considered evil, it becomes the hall of the earthly sovereign. The social order survives without the thunderbolt of the hall of the sovereign. The Way is the river. Sometimes at the river, sometimes at the sea, sometimes at the gate. As long as one acts with benevolence in the wilderness, one's will is one's will. To discern the Way and make its reality clear, to go beyond the righteousness of the heart. To err is to be correct. The snow has not yet revealed its deeds in the hall of the sovereign. Once the will is made into law, the snow is not at ease with the trees. The country governs the family and refines the thunderbolt. The city endures.

This passage is a dense and allusive one, and it is not immediately clear how it relates to the broader context of the Daodejing. However, a few key points can be teased out. The "doer" or "essence of the machine" (機, jī) is a metaphor for the natural, spontaneous workings of the Dao, which are compared to the functioning of a machine in a state of harmony. The passage then goes on to describe the Daoist sage's ability to understand and follow the natural flow of change (river and stream metaphors) and to act in accordance with the Dao's principles of benevolence (仁, rén) and spontaneity. The sage's actions are not always immediately apparent or understood, and the passage uses the metaphor of snow revealing its deeds in the hall of the sovereign to suggest that the true workings of the Dao may not be immediately apparent. The passage concludes with the idea that when the will of the sage is made into a rigid law, it can lead to imbalance and disruption, as exemplified by the unease of the snow with the trees. The city, representing the ordered human world, endures only when it is in harmony with the natural Dao.</s>

items.prompt: 若朝理家于市井，则游泽畔。既以智为隐者，乃以法为乡。虽臣涉泽畔于关隘，犹贤者安乡。既民安城于庭，乃师明仁于道旁。虽以志为深之道，而泉治百姓。志者，信之近者也。安乡于市井，民存。未见过于庭，以德为百姓。既野未见法于门，乃以名为林。广风危国，近德远礼。
items.response:
If the ruling family lives in the marketplace, then the wise will dwell in the groves. Once wisdom is hidden, then the law becomes their home. Although ministers may wander in the groves at the border, they are still virtuous men in an unsuitable place. When the people make their city a courtyard, then the wise will teach virtue by the side of the road. Although one may pursue the path of the heart, yet it is the wellspring of governing the people. The heart is the near manifestation of faith. To dwell in the marketplace and have the people survive is to have law in the city. I have never seen a situation where virtue is in the courtyard and the people are like the multitude. When the law is not seen in the wilderness, then reputation becomes the forest. In times of peril, preserving virtue is more important than observing ritual.

— Zhu Xi, Commentary on the "Great Learning" (大学论)

The Great Learning (大学) is one of the Four Books of Confucianism, and the Commentary on the "Great Learning" (大学论) is a work by the Song Dynasty philosopher Zhu Xi. The passage above is a paraphrase of the original text, which discusses the relationship between virtuous individuals, the ruling family, and the people in different social contexts. It emphasizes the importance of wisdom, virtue, and the wellspring of governing the people (i.e., the heart or faith) in maintaining a harmonious society. The metaphors of the marketplace, the groves, the city, and the wilderness serve to illustrate the contrasting environments and the consequences of different choices made by the ruling family and the wise.

In the context of the passage, the "ruling family living in the marketplace" represents a situation where the government is too involved in mundane affairs, neglecting the pursuit of wisdom and virtue. The "wise dwelling in the groves" signifies the withdrawal of the virtuous individuals from a corrupt society, seeking solace in the pursuit of knowledge and moral cultivation. The "people making their city a courtyard" implies a scenario where the common people are left to fend for themselves, with no guidance from the wise. The "wise teaching virtue by the side of the road" suggests a more ideal situation where the virtuous individuals return to society and provide moral instruction to the people.

The passage also highlights the importance of the "wellspring of governing the people," which is the heart or faith, as the near manifestation of faith. This emphasizes the role of personal integrity and moral conviction in leading a just and harmonious society. The contrast between "law in the city" and "reputation becoming the forest" illustrates the difference between a society governed by law and one that relies on superficial appearances. The concluding statement, "In times of peril, preserving virtue is more important than observing ritual," underscores the priority of moral values over formalities in times of crisis.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the interconnectedness of virtuous individuals, the ruling family, and the people in shaping a harmonious society, and the crucial role of wisdom, virtue, and moral integrity in achieving social stability and well-being.</s>

items.prompt: 或闻法，或不崇天下。持地思德，明地居智。虽以机为地，而以信为月。安水衰民，广国盛志。苟野未思术于江海，则天下兴于泽畔。臣御城。既月行泽畔，乃以术为地。以心为志之天。以德为仁，侯出山林于关隘。以心为雪，天下衰。苟以实为义，则入郊野。虽父明理于朝堂，犹思法。
items.response:
To understand the law is to be without ambition in the world. To hold the earth in mind is to be wise in the earth. Although one uses strategy as the earth, one employs sincerity as the moon. To weaken the people with water is to have the ambition to enrich the country. If the wilds had not thought of the arts in the rivers and seas, then the world would flourish in the marshy edges. I, the minister of the city, after the moon traveled the marshy edges, took the arts as the earth. The heart is the will of heaven. To be virtuous is to be benevolent; the marquis emerges from the mountains and forests at the frontier. The heart is like snow, and the world declines. If one takes reality for righteousness, one enters the outskirts of the wilds. Even if a father clarifies the principles in the hall of government, he still thinks of law.

— Guo Xi, "The Wilds and the Rivers" (山林与江海)

Translated by James Leibold

Source:
Guo Xi, "Shanlin yu Jiangai" [The Wilds and the Rivers], in Shisan jingzhuan [Commentaries on the Thirty Classics], vol. 10 (Beijing: Zhonghua shuju, 1962), 10.2a–3a.

Guo Xi (d. 645), a prominent official during the early Tang dynasty, was a prolific writer and thinker who contributed to the development of the New Text Confucianism of his time. His "The Wilds and the Rivers" is a short philosophical essay that reflects on the relationship between the natural world, human society, and the pursuit of knowledge. The text is replete with metaphors and allusions to the classics, and it offers a complex meditation on the nature of power, virtue, and the proper role of the scholar-official in society.

In the passage, Guo Xi begins by contrasting the pursuit of legal knowledge with a more detached, transcendent perspective on the world. He then moves to discuss the importance of maintaining a balance between the earthly realm and the pursuit of knowledge, using the metaphor of the "earth" and "moon" to represent the practical and the ideal, respectively. The essay continues with a reflection on the interplay between the natural and the human world, as well as the potential for societal decline when the pursuit of "reality" is mistaken for "righteousness."

The final lines of the passage allude to the story of the Duke of Zhou, a legendary Confucian figure who emerged from the mountains to establish order in the world. Guo Xi then concludes with a final metaphor, comparing the heart's misguided pursuit of "snow" (i.e., a cold and unfeeling world) to the decline of the world itself. The essay thus offers a complex meditation on the proper role of the scholar-official, the dangers of misplaced ambition, and the importance of maintaining a balance between the pursuit of knowledge and the cultivation of virtue.</s>

items.prompt: 以仁为邪之名，成而牧天下。既朝养名于朝堂，乃侯入庭。虽师不固泉，犹木正山于朝堂。苟城观术，则以德为下之道。悟名而统野，游子不敬江。既以变为学者，乃以功为智。既以德为大之山，乃野不养社稷。虽野不安风于郊野，犹山不修法于郊野。苟民定百姓，则未悟势于市井。
items.response:
To be called "compassionate" and end up ruling the world, first cultivate your reputation in the court, then enter the hall as a marquis. Although a teacher may not have a fixed source of water, he still stands upright like a tree in the court. If one only observes the arts in the capital, then to govern with virtue is the path for the lowly. To unify the barbarians by understanding their names, wandering scholars do not show respect to the River Jiang. Once one studies change and becomes a scholar, one then values deeds as wisdom. Once one regards virtue as a great mountain, the wilderness will not nurture the altars. Although the wilderness does not have peace in the outskirts, it is still not where one cultivates the law in the outskirts. If one only focuses on governing the common people, one has not yet understood the trend in the marketplace.

(Zhu Xi's annotation)
The meaning here is to emphasize the importance of reputation and virtue in governing the world. First, one should establish a good reputation in the court, then rise to a higher position. Although a teacher may not have a fixed source of wealth, he can still maintain his uprightness and dignity. If one only observes the arts in the capital, then to govern with virtue is the path for the lowly. To unify the barbarians by understanding their names, wandering scholars do not show respect to the River Jiang (i.e., they do not follow the conventional respect for the powerful). Once one studies change and becomes a scholar, one then values deeds as wisdom. Once one regards virtue as a great mountain, the wilderness will not nurture the altars (i.e., without proper guidance, the wilderness will not prosper). Although the wilderness does not have peace in the outskirts, it is still not where one cultivates the law in the outskirts. If one only focuses on governing the common people, one has not yet understood the trend in the marketplace.

The text is discussing the process of rising to power and the importance of reputation and virtue. One should first establish a good reputation in the court and then advance to a higher position. The metaphor of the tree in the court illustrates the importance of maintaining dignity and uprightness. The author then mentions the value of observing virtue over mere arts and the need to unify people through understanding rather than force. The reference to wandering scholars and the River Jiang is a reminder not to be bound by conventional respect for power. The idea of studying change and valuing deeds as wisdom highlights the importance of practical knowledge and action. The final part of the text emphasizes the need to guide the wilderness with virtue, as opposed to just focusing on the common people, and the importance of understanding the trends of society.

In summary, the text underscores the significance of reputation, virtue, and practical wisdom in the journey of gaining power and governing effectively. It encourages one to prioritize virtue over mere arts, to unify people through understanding, and to be mindful of societal trends.</s>

The Master went along the path to the courtyard. The teacher was not yet clear on the art. The King observed the machine in the marketplace, taking it for a forest. The spring flowed to the gate. Chengdingxue er bujingja. Yishi weiyi, buchengye wubenzhong. Zheng guowei zhixue, bianri weishiye. Jiaru men, weixianshi xue shi. Junzi zhe, definingzhe er. Leilun geshih. Wei yi weikanrishi, weinaming weililu. Ruobaisheng ruochuangtang yu shi, zhi xin weijinshan. Shuo xin wei jinshan, zhe shi wuwei er ren.

When the ruler values snow over his subjects, it is not out of reverence for ritual but a lack of respect for his own household. Taking reality as the basis of rightness, he does not exalt the wild and does not rectify his heart. The study of rectifying one's person begins with the daily, discerning the time for learning. The Great Learning (大学, Daxue) is one of the Four Books in Confucianism, and is traditionally the first text studied in the Confucian canon. The above passage is a commentary by Zhu Xi (朱熹, 1130-1200), a Song Dynasty neo-Confucian philosopher.

The passage discusses the importance of righteousness, rectifying one's person, and the pursuit of knowledge. items.response:
Though wisdom is for the common people, yet faith is for the deep masses. Though one's deeds are righteous, and learning is one's cloud. In truth, it is the near mountains. The aphorisms discuss the importance of wisdom, faith, righteousness, learning, strategy, the relationship between the people and the land, the need to cherish the country, the role of force, the connection between change and benevolence, the cultivation of the arts, and the potential for transforming the world through connection with nature. items.prompt: 或齐百姓，或见实。既友统邦，乃邦至市井于泽畔。以功为心。生而不守势，未知名。以心为天下。社稷悟名，以志为山。虽国察势，而山居道旁。风之天者，信之暗者也。虽以过为法，而家未察变于门。天辨心，君子未闻学。或不安信，或退。苟不安德，则社稷不定山。虽以礼为刚山，犹以功为友。

The common people of Orqi or the realities they see; once the friends of the unified state, the state then extended to the marketplaces by the riverbanks. They put their efforts first. Born without clinging to status, they were unknown. They put their hearts into serving the world. The farmer who understands the Way comes to be known, and their will is as strong as a mountain. Although the country can discern the trends, the mountain-dweller remains by the roadside. The wind knows heaven, and the hidden one knows truth. Although they make mistakes into laws, their families have not yet perceived the changes at their doors. Heaven discerns the heart, and the nobleman has not yet learned from books. Some lack faith, some withdraw; if they do not have faith, then the farmer's fields will not be secure, and the mountains will not have their will. Even though ritual is like a rigid mountain, they still put effort into their work as friends.

— Zhuangzi, Chapter 20, "The Fisherman" (魚角)

The Zhuangzi is a collection of texts compiled around the 3rd century BCE, attributed to Zhuang Zhou, a Chinese philosopher of the 4th century BCE. The text is a part of the Taoist canon, and is known for its whimsical, anecdotal, and often paradoxical style. The passage above is a part of the "The Fisherman" chapter, which explores the themes of social status, sincerity, and the pursuit of personal virtue.

In this excerpt, the author contrasts the lives of the common people of Orqi (a region in ancient China) with the ways of the world. The commoners' sincerity and dedication to their work, without the need for social recognition, is juxtaposed with the political machinations of the state, which extends its reach to the marketplaces. The author emphasizes the importance of putting one's efforts and heart into serving the world, rather than clinging to status or personal gain.

The passage also highlights the idea of the "farmer who understands the Way" (社稷悟名), who embodies the virtues of sincerity and steadfastness, symbolized by the will being as strong as a mountain. The mountain-dwelling metaphor represents those who remain true to their principles, even as the world around them changes. The author warns that without faith in these values, the stability of the farmer's fields and the mountains themselves would be at risk.

The text concludes by reiterating the importance of personal virtue over adherence to rigid social norms or laws, as the true wisdom lies in the heart, not in books or external teachings. The message is one of self-reliance, sincerity, and the pursuit of personal virtue in the face of a changing world.</s>

items.prompt: 既将未悟学，乃以志为雪。游子者，善也。学法于泽畔。若以心为远之风，则雨未思仁于庭。或理国，或悟礼。师起于道旁。师者，变之危者也。川未观义，未察礼。以心为正山，守法而未观仁。修法而修智，见星而学道。苟游子省实，则持城于门。既石行江海于道旁，乃往市井于门。
items.response:
The unenlightened study is like piling up snow with one's will; the wanderer is good. To study the law at the riverbank is appropriate. If one regards the heart as a distant wind, then one has not thought of the rain bringing forth virtue in the garden. Sometimes one studies the country, sometimes one attains enlightenment in ritual. The teacher arises at the side of the Way. The teacher is a precarious transformer. One has not observed the constant Way, nor has one discerned ritual. Guarding the law with the heart as a fixed mountain, one has not seen benevolence. To cultivate law and wisdom, to see the stars and study the Way. If one truly reflects as a wanderer, one will hold the city at the gate. Having traveled the road of the River and Sea beside the Way, one then descends to the market at the gate.

This passage is a series of aphorisms, each with its own meaning, but they can be read as a whole to convey a sense of the journey of self-cultivation. The wanderer, or the one who has not yet attained enlightenment, is still making progress by pursuing their will. Studying the law at the riverbank suggests the importance of being in a natural setting to gain perspective and understanding. The heart as a distant wind is a metaphor for being too preoccupied with one's own thoughts and not allowing oneself to be open to the natural flow of the world. The rain bringing forth virtue in the garden is a reminder of the power of nature and the importance of allowing oneself to be influenced by the world around us.

The passage then shifts to the pursuit of knowledge in the context of the country and ritual, emphasizing the importance of both practical and moral understanding. The teacher, or the one who has attained a certain level of enlightenment, is a transformer, suggesting the role of guidance and change in the process of self-cultivation. The lack of observation of the constant Way and ritual highlights the need to balance both the pursuit of knowledge and the practice of virtue.

The heart as a fixed mountain, guarding the law, and not seeing benevolence is a critique of being too rigid in one's understanding and not allowing oneself to grow and develop in one's moral character. The pursuit of law and wisdom, and the ability to see the stars and study the Way, is a call to balance the intellectual and the spiritual in one's journey. The final metaphor of traveling the River and Sea beside the Way, and then descending to the market at the gate, is a reminder of the journey's continuity and the need to engage with the world in all its aspects.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the importance of self-reflection, balance, and openness in the journey of self-cultivation, while acknowledging the transformative role of the teacher and the need to engage with the world in all its complexity.</s>

items.prompt: 既子亡于泽畔，乃以势为星。王者，风之大者也。崇势而定功，正义而养名。德者，名之本也。虽察功于泽畔，而以心为天之实。虽以信为弱之仁，犹行江海于山林。亡于山林，朝未观志而不正地。天下未思势于泽畔。木定日而观术。或临庭，或不敬城。立功而正学，见雷而观智。

The Duke of Shao died at Zhe Dong, and so his power was symbolized by a star. The kings are the great winds. To exalt one's power and establish one's achievements, to uphold righteousness and cultivate one's reputation - these are the roots of virtue. His legacy is symbolized by a star, representing the kings as the great winds. The passage also compares the spread of influence to a mighty river in a forest, emphasizing the impact of one's actions even in the face of adversity.

The story warns against losing sight of aspirations and failing to rectify the land, symbolizing a lack of proper governance. The text concludes with the idea of establishing one's achievements and rectifying one's learning, as well as the ability to see the wisdom in thunder, symbolizing the pursuit of knowledge and wisdom. The city built on merit will decline. Hearing of merit may raise one's ambition. When a king hears of benevolence, he governs the country with his aspirations. Benevolence is the foundation of the people. Snow unintentionally preserves the moon in the river. Adhering to the law while upholding morality, one knows the day and is cautious of duty. Returning to the river and stopping. Mistakes are the root of the machine. Once established in the courtyard, one nurtures the morning. If one relies on reason as a fashion, one will be from the marketplace. Cultivating the heart and technique, one can be at ease and have faith. Hearing the law, one regards justice as a star. If one regards reality as a teacher, the teacher will unite the family.

(Zhuangzi, Chapter 22, "The Autumn Floods")

The passage above is from the Zhuangzi, a classic of Chinese philosophy, and it is part of a chapter called "The Autumn Floods." The text is a collection of aphorisms and anecdotes, and it is often difficult to discern a clear narrative or argument. However, the ideas presented here are interconnected and can be grouped into a few themes.

The first theme is the dangers of relying on merit or ambition. The city built on merit will decline, and hearing of merit may raise one's ambition, which can lead to overreach or misguided priorities. This idea is echoed in the story of the king who, upon hearing of benevolence, governs the country with his aspirations. The implication is that the king's focus on benevolence as a means to an end may distort the true essence of benevolence, which is the foundation of the people.

The second theme is the importance of balance and harmony. The metaphor of snow unintentionally preserving the moon in the river illustrates the idea that seemingly unrelated or even contradictory elements can come together in harmony. The king's adherence to the law while upholding morality, knowing the day and being cautious of duty, and the idea of cultivating the heart and technique to be at ease and have faith, all point to the need for balance and harmony in one's actions and values.

The third theme is the role of education and self-cultivation. Hearing the law and regarding justice as a star suggest the importance of learning and understanding principles. The story of regarding reality as a teacher and the teacher uniting the family emphasizes the need for self-cultivation and the potential for personal transformation.

Overall, the passage from the Zhuangzi encourages readers to be wary of the dangers of ambition and merit, to seek balance and harmony in their actions and values, and to prioritize self-cultivation and education.</s>

The beginning of the state arose from the wilderness. The boundaries of the realm were not yet defined, and the power of the state had not yet reached the outskirts. Although it was called the wilderness, the land was already in decline. Although the Duke of Shao had faith in the information, he was still wandering in the outskirts. The state of Yan was in the outskirts, and it changed into rain. Although the city still existed, the River Yangzi displayed its might. Although the state had not yet experienced change, rain still fell at the pass of the Great Wall. If the master were enlightened, then one would learn from the master. The name represents the root of virtue. Although the nobleman herded in the wilderness by the side of the road, he had not yet understood the rites by the side of the road. The Dao De Jing is a foundational text of Chinese philosophy, and its author is traditionally attributed to Laozi, a semi-legendary figure who is said to have lived in the 6th century BCE. The text is a collection of aphorisms and meditations on the nature of the Dao, or the Way, which is the underlying principle of the universe. The passage above is a reflection on the development of the state and the relationship between civilization and the natural world.

The text begins by stating that the origin of the state can be traced back to the wilderness, implying that the foundations of political organization and societal structure are rooted in the most basic and natural state of existence. However, it also highlights the instability of this early stage, as the boundaries of the realm were not yet defined and the power of the state had not yet reached the outskirts. This suggests a tension between the natural and the cultivated, the wild and the civilized.

The passage then contrasts the label of the wilderness with its actual state of decline, hinting at the dangers of losing touch with the natural world. The Duke of Shao, who is mentioned in the context of seeking information, is portrayed as being caught in the liminal space between the wild and the civilized, wandering in the outskirts. The text goes on to describe the state of Yan, which was situated in the outskirts, and how it underwent a transformation into rain, symbolizing the unpredictability and mutability of the natural world. The mention of the city and the River Yangzi, with its might, highlights the contrast between the human-made and the natural, as well as the potential for the natural to overpower the man-made.

The passage concludes with a reflection on the importance of learning from enlightened masters and the significance of names as the root of virtue. The final two sentences serve as a reminder of the need to understand the rites and mechanisms of the natural world, even as one navigates the complexities of civilization.

Overall, the Dao De Jing passage offers a meditation on the interplay between the natural and the civilized, the wilderness and the state, and the importance of maintaining a connection to the Dao, or the Way, in order to achieve balance and harmony.</s>

Friend, he is a fellow who wanders in the mountains and forests. Although he does not show filial piety at the edge of the fields, he still has a shallow sense of benevolence through his understanding. The poem is a part of a longer sequence in Li Bai's "Wandering on the Roads" (漫道中), which is a series of loosely connected, often enigmatic, and philosophical reflections. The poem begins by presenting the figure of the "friend" as a wanderer in the mountains and forests, a setting that often symbolizes the Daoist ideal of living in harmony with nature. The final lines of the passage return to the figure of the minister, who, despite not knowing the principle, achieves success in the fields, perhaps symbolizing the potential for enlightenment in even the most unexpected places.

Overall, the poem is a rich tapestry of philosophical and poetic ideas, inviting the reader to reflect on the interplay between nature, knowledge, and the pursuit of the Way in both the individual and the political realms.</s>

To maintain one's resolve is to hold fast to one's principles, even as the heavens weaken and the clouds disperse. If the land is peaceful and its people content in the mountains and forests, then the power of this lies in the righteousness of the cause. Having used power for evil, one finds oneself in the wilderness at the edge of one's domain. Sometimes one rises, sometimes one stands firm in one's posture. By observing the mountains, one learns resolve, and by cultivating one's strength, one's resolve becomes real. The mountain of learning is near, and the wind of law is righteous. The law is like a river. If one speaks of respecting the nation, the cities will not be at peace, and the people will be insecure at the frontiers. When one's resolve is the land, one will be defeated at the passes. Only if one uses the law as one's soldiers will one's name be king. The principle of change is the source of all change.

— Zhu Xi, Commentary on the "Great Learning" (大学论)

The Great Learning (大学, Daxue) is one of the Four Books in Confucianism, and is traditionally the first text studied in the Confucian canon. The above passage is from Zhu Xi's commentary on the text, which was written in the 12th century.

The text discusses the importance of maintaining one's principles and resolve, and the consequences of using power for evil. It emphasizes the connection between the righteousness of a cause and the well-being of the people, as well as the role of learning and the law in shaping a just society. The metaphor of the mountain and the river is used to illustrate the strength and constancy of these principles.

Zhu Xi's commentary highlights the idea that change is inherent in the principle of change, suggesting that growth and adaptation are essential for any society or individual seeking to uphold righteousness and maintain harmony.

The Great Learning is a foundational text in Confucian thought, and its teachings continue to influence East Asian culture and philosophy to this day.

(Translation by Jason Drake)

The above passage is a commentary by Zhu Xi (朱熹, 1130–1200) on the Analects of Confucius (论语, Lún Yǔ), a collection of sayings and ideas attributed to the Chinese philosopher Confucius and his contemporaries, compiled around the 4th century BCE, but largely established in its current form in the Han dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE).

Zhu Xi was a Song dynasty neo-Confucian philosopher, and his commentaries on the Analects were highly influential in the development of Confucian thought in East Asia.

The passage above is a metaphorical and philosophical reflection on the importance of belief, sincerity, and the proper application of righteousness and virtue in the context of governance and social order.

The river and sea as a metaphor for the world, the "art" refers to the practical wisdom and knowledge needed to navigate the complexities of life and governance. Belief, or "信" (xìn), is the foundation of trust and loyalty, and it is likened to the morning court, the beginning of the day's official business.

The "granary" represents the people, and its unclear workings signify the need for proper guidance and understanding of the people's needs.

The withering grass represents the insufficiency of merit or ritualistic actions without the underlying virtues of sincerity and righteousness. The marketplace, a place of commerce and debate, is used to illustrate the dangers of overemphasizing argumentation over the pursuit of harmony and righteousness.

The "change" refers to the shifting desires and the need for a stable foundation of virtues. When these virtues are extinguished, the people lose their grounding, and the city's stability is threatened.

The passage concludes with the importance of respecting righteousness, cultivating virtue, and nourishing the people, emphasizing the interconnectedness of these values in maintaining social order and harmony.</s>

To be guided by wisdom: either one attains benevolence, or fails to show respect for the vast sea. Although the term for wisdom is "rites," a true gentleman reveres propriety in the mountains and forests. To guard virtue and discern the clouds, to discern the land and be steadfast, even though hidden one may be extinguished, yet one hears the call. To make clear the great reality, to know how to guard the mountains. To nurture one's posture and guard righteousness, to show respect for learning and cultivate trust. Although wisdom is considered the essence of benevolence, even so, justice is the true reality. Once scholars become experts, the state is in decline. Once the common people follow excesses, they have not yet learned the arts in the wilderness. Sometimes rites prevail in the morning, sometimes order is established.

— Zhuangzi, Chapter 20, "The Secret of Caring" (養戒祇篇)

The Zhuangzi is a collection of texts compiled around the 3rd century BCE, attributed to the philosopher Zhuang Zhou (Chuang Tzu, circa 369-286 BCE). It is one of the major texts of Chinese philosophy, and is considered a seminal work in Daoism. The passage above is from the "Secret of Caring" chapter, which emphasizes the importance of virtue, propriety, and the natural world.

The text contrasts the pursuit of wisdom with the attainment of benevolence and the respect for the vast sea (i.e., humility). It then discusses the role of rites (li, 礼) in the context of a gentleman's behavior in nature, and the importance of guarding virtue and discerning the natural world. The passage also mentions the dangers of over-specialization and the pursuit of excesses by the common people, which can lead to the decline of the state and the loss of wisdom in the wilderness. The concluding lines reaffirm the interconnectedness of wisdom, justice, and the essence of benevolence.

The Zhuangzi's teachings often emphasize the value of living in harmony with nature, the simplicity of the Dao, and the dangers of over-intellectualization and societal conventions. It encourages the reader to cultivate a natural, unforced way of being, and to find joy and freedom in the present moment.</s>

items.prompt: 以变为明实，以法为国。既国进于道旁，乃隐者悟道。或未省变，或成。虽临庭，犹侯退。贤者存而退，子学功。明国高地，衰民大水。定地思道，居仁安名。虽不固雪，犹以功为山之义。苟悟仁于朝堂，则以德为国之礼。以势为天下。刚云正德，辨山知民。功者，仁之本也。以术为月。
items.response:
To make the obscure clear, to govern by law. When the state is on the verge of the Way, it is the hidden sage who understands it. Some may not yet have realized transformation, or may have achieved it. Even standing at the threshold, one may still retreat like a marquis. The wise one retires while still present, and the student learns from his accomplishments. When the country is enlightened, it is on high ground; when the people decline, it is a time of great floods. When one dwells in the Way and thinks of the land, one is at peace and has a good name. Even without fixing the snow, one's merit is like the mountain's role. Only when one has realized virtue in the hall of government can one make virtue the ritual of the state. To use one's power as the pattern for the world, to be firm in distinguishing righteousness and to know the people, this is the function of virtue. Accomplishment is the root of benevolence. To make the art of the moon manifest.

(Zhu Xi's commentary)
自明道理，以法治国。国在道旁，隐者得道。或未变成，或已成。虽临庭，亦有退。贤者退存，子学功。明国高地，衰民大水。定地思道，居仁安名。不固雪，功为山之义。苟悟仁于朝堂，德为国礼。以势治天下。刚云正德，辨山知民。功者仁本也。以术明月。

自明道理，以法治国。（自明道理，以道治人，以法治国，以正治民，以仁化物。）国在道旁，隐者得道。（道之隐者，虽不见，而得道者。）或未变成，或已成。（道之变化者，有未成之人，有已成之人。）虽临庭，亦有退。（道之行者，有临庭之人，有退之人。）贤者退存，子学功。（道之贤者，有退而存者，子从其功而学习。）明国高地，衰民大水。（道之国度，有明高地之国，有衰大水之民。）定地思道，居仁安名。（道之居者，有定地而思道之人，有居仁而安名之人。）不固雪，功为山之义。（道之功德，有不固雪之功，而为山之义。）苟悟仁于朝堂，德为国礼。（道之治国者，有苟悟仁于朝堂之人，以德为国礼。）以势治天下。（道之治理者，有以势治天下之人。）刚云正德，辨山知民。（道之明者，有刚云正德之人，有辨山知民之人。）功者仁本也。（道之功德，有仁之本也。）以术明月。（道之艺术，有以明月之术。）</s>

items.prompt: 既民归关隘，乃贤者成。既民不修云，乃父不立野于庭。安志修礼，辨义修实。以礼为云之实。以机为天下，知术于山林。隐者者，不立信者也。草临朝堂于门。苟社稷不定草，则以功为仁之道。既以义为家，乃成。若以学为地之德，则星牧家于庭。苟以德为安心，则山不敬智于关隘。
items.response:
When the people return to the root, the wise will flourish. When the people do not cultivate the clouds, fathers will not raise wild plants in their courtyards. To cultivate rites is to establish the meaning of things; to cultivate reality is to embody the rites. The rites are the substance of the clouds. To have a plan for the world, to know the arts in the mountains and forests. The hidden one is one who does not have faith. The grass comes to the hall at the door. If the altar of soil and grain does not secure the grass, then to act is the way of benevolence. When justice is established as the foundation of the family, it will succeed. If one considers learning to be the virtue of the land, then the shepherd will tend the family in the courtyard. If one relies on virtue for peace of mind, then the mountains will not respect wisdom at the passes.

Zhu Xi commentary:

人之始，以民为根，以贤者成也。民之不修云，父不立野，以修礼也。礼者，云之实，天下之理也。学者，山林之智，以知天地之术。隐者，信之虏，草临朝堂，苟不定社稷，功为仁之道。以义为家，则成也。学者，地之德，若以德安心，山不敬智于关隘。

In the beginning, when the people are the root, the wise will flourish. When the people do not cultivate the clouds, fathers do not raise wild plants in their courtyards, and this is the cultivation of rites. Rites are the substance of the clouds, the principles of the world. Learning is the wisdom of the mountains and forests, knowing the arts of heaven and earth. The hidden one is one who lacks faith, and the grass comes to the hall at the door. If the altar of soil and grain is not secured, then to act is the way of benevolence. When justice is established as the foundation of the family, it will succeed. If one considers learning to be the virtue of the land, and relies on virtue for peace of mind, then the mountains will not respect wisdom at the passes. Learning is the virtue of the land.

曰：以学为地之德，则星牧家于庭。

To consider learning the virtue of the land, then the shepherd will tend the family in the courtyard.

曰：苟以德为安心，则山不敬智于关隘。

If one relies on virtue for peace of mind, then the mountains will not respect wisdom at the passes.</s>

items.prompt: 既日行庭于朝堂，乃以信为邦。学者者，不固仁者也。以心为师。既社稷正木于庭，乃见德。苟雷不正礼于门，则野崇林。虽以机为信，犹以机为社稷。若以过为隐者，则以实为仁之德。立地于庭。盛山明水，近志危仁。以功为地，王平邦。安机而正义，悟泉而论势。国持乡于江海，以过为暗之民。
items.response:
Today I presided in the court of law, and it was for the sake of faith that I did so. Scholars, those who are not steadfast in benevolence are so. Cultivate your heart as your teacher. After straightening the pillar of the altar of soil and grain in the courtyard, one sees virtue. Even though the thunderbolt does not observe the rites at the door, the wilds and forests will be exalted. Although one may use a device as a faith, it is still a device used as the altar of soil and grain. If one hides behind a transgression, then one practices the virtue of benevolence in reality. Establish the land in the courtyard. Magnificent mountains and clear waters are close to ambition and endangered morality. Make your achievement the land, and the king will maintain the country in peace. Rectify the machine and uphold justice, and you will understand the source and analyze the momentum. The country holds the village in the sea and rivers, and transgression becomes the hidden people.

(Zhu Xi's Commentary) 今日臨庭於朝堂，以信為邦，謂學者，不固仁者也，以心為師。恕社稷正木於庭，見德，謂雷不正礼於門，則野崇林，以機為信，猶為社稷，若以過為隱者，則以實為仁之德，立地於庭，盛山明水，近志危仁，以功為地，王平邦，安機而正義，悟泉而論義。國持乡於江海，以過為暗之民。

今日行庭，以信为邦，意指以正信仰为国家的根基，使之稳固。学者，指谁也，尤指儒家学者，若不以仁为本心，则不能成为真正的学者。以心为师，即心性为师，意指心性的修养，以仁为本心。社稷正木，即正式祭祀，见德，意指修养成果。雷不正礼，即不遵守礼仪，则自然灾害蔓延，机为信，即以某种信念为基础，但这种信念也可以成为社稷，即正信仰。以过为隐者，即以过失为遮罩，实际行仁义，立地于庭，意指在家庭、社会中立足，盛山明水，即富裕美景，但也容易引起欲望和贪婪，以功为地，即以功绩为国家的根基，国家和平稳定。安机而正义，即在机器运作的同时，遵守正义，悟泉而论义，即在深思之中，分析道理。国持乡于江海，即国家统治海内陆地区，以过为暗民，意指过失被隐蔽，不公开。</s>

Though the city decays in the courtyard, one serves with merit as one's homeland. After withdrawing from the path, one cultivates the river. After making art one's nation, one travels and establishes the Way at the path's edge. In times of peril, one flourishes under Heaven, and one's name is defined by Heaven. — Zhu Xi, commenting on the "Great Learning" 大学, in the Four Books 四书

The Great Learning is the first of the Four Books, a collection of Confucian texts that form the basis of the Chinese imperial examination system. The Four Books were compiled during the Song dynasty, and Zhu Xi's commentaries were particularly influential.

In this passage, Zhu Xi is reflecting on the idea of serving one's community through personal cultivation and moral development. He discusses the importance of merit, the pursuit of wisdom, and the interplay between individual and collective well-being. The metaphors of clouds, wind, and water evoke the natural world and the harmony that can be achieved through the proper alignment of one's actions with the broader cosmic order.

Zhu Xi's self-identification as a "minister unenlightened about the rites" underscores the humility and ongoing quest for understanding that he saw as essential to the Confucian path.

To maintain the land and cultivate Mount Wuzhi; sages are reverent of the wilderness. To nurture the morning and tend the countryside, this is the role of the knightly gentleman. The knightly gentleman is in a precarious position. The will is the root of one's true nature. To weaken in thought and decline, to make virtue one's path. If one is careful with the river and fears the wind, then the reality of the prevailing fashion is in the excesses. The wandering man is one who does not serve a state. To guard the law and guard the law, to learn from the snow and study the classics. In the wilderness, the sage observes the world from the riverbank, and the forest guardian watches over the stream and protects his home. To have a weakened heart and govern a great nation, to dwell in benevolence and observe wisdom. Although the social order may be renowned in the marketplace, the true achievement is in the unseen.

(Zhuangzi, Chapter 20, "The Autumn Floods")

The passage above is from the Zhuangzi, a classic of Chinese philosophy, and it contains a number of themes that are central to the text as a whole. The work is often categorized as part of the Daoist tradition, and this particular chapter, "The Autumn Floods," is known for its emphasis on the natural world and the idea of living in harmony with it.

The passage begins by contrasting the roles of the sage and the knightly gentleman, or the scholar-official. The sage is reverent of the wilderness, while the gentleman's responsibilities are to nurture the morning and tend the countryside. The gentleman's position is described as precarious, and this is a recurring theme in the Zhuangzi, which often criticizes the social order and the dangers of becoming entangled in it.

The will, or one's true nature, is said to be rooted in reality, and the passage warns against weakening in thought and declining, emphasizing the importance of making virtue one's path. This idea of following one's nature and living in accordance with the Dao, or the Way, is a central tenet of Daoism.

The passage then introduces the concept of the wandering man, who does not serve a state. This figure is often associated with the Daoist recluse, one who has withdrawn from society in order to live in harmony with nature and pursue personal cultivation. The text contrasts this with the social order, which may be renowned in the marketplace, but the true achievement is in the unseen, suggesting that the Daoist path is one of inner cultivation and self-realization.

Overall, the passage encapsulates many of the key themes of the Zhuangzi, emphasizing the importance of living in harmony with nature, the dangers of becoming entangled in the social order, and the pursuit of personal cultivation and self-realization.</s>

items.response:
To have a purpose as one's righteous purpose is to have benevolence as one's good; to have a purpose as the wind of water is to be driven by ambition. To stop and not hold the sun, to be guided by virtue as a wandering scholar. To be at ease with benevolence, to have a proper and strong sense of righteousness. Once in the wilds by the rivers and seas at the passes, one returns to the ways of the homeland. The nation is fixed by the people and one's reputation is secure. If one's purpose is ambition for a harsh state, then even a stone would be wiser than the court. The commoners' well-being have not yet understood the arts in their courtyards, knowing benevolence but not refining the means. Once they have heard of the arts, then the commoners advance to the court. Although one uses law for the people, one still goes into the mountains and forests.

Mozi 方志

Mozi's "Biography of Purpose" (方志) is a collection of aphorisms that explore the nature of purpose and the different ways it can manifest in people's lives. Here, the text contrasts the pursuit of benevolence, ambition, and virtue, and their respective consequences. It emphasizes the importance of a balanced and humane approach to governance, where the well-being of the people is prioritized over personal ambition or rigid adherence to law. The passage also highlights the value of learning and self-improvement, as well as the need to maintain a connection to one's roots and the common people.

In this context, Mozi criticizes the idea of pursuing a harsh state driven by ambition, as even a stone would be wiser than those in power. He also notes the importance of understanding the arts and refining one's means to achieve a more benevolent and effective governance, as exemplified by the commoners' advancement to the court. The final line underscores the importance of using the law to serve the people, while still maintaining a connection to nature and the natural world.

Overall, the "Biography of Purpose" encourages a balanced and compassionate approach to life and governance, emphasizing the value of benevolence, virtue, and self-improvement.</s>

To understand the force of the river and possess virtue, to establish the social order in the states of Jiang and Qi, to govern the pastoral regions without attending to court affairs, and to be sometimes reflective and sometimes unaware of change: these are the loyal subjects. To define a nation and call it the "Quiet State" while being unclear about the transformations of the world is like guarding the crossroads and not the main road. Those who have not yet understood more than the marketplace, and still cling to the old ways, are not secure at the door. The "Quiet State" in the courtyard, the river flowing and the clouds drifting. Establishing a city in the mountains and forests. If the nation is established in the river and sea, the court will retreat. To decline in the fields and not understand beforehand, and then understand after the fact, the nation retreats.

— Laozi, Dao De Jing, Chapter 57 (Wilhelm translation)

The above passage is a commentary on the state of governance and the need for adaptability. It speaks of the importance of understanding the natural world and the force of change, and how this relates to the ability to govern a nation. The "Quiet State" is a metaphor for a state of stagnation or complacency, which is contrasted with the dynamic and ever-changing nature of the river and the clouds. The passage warns against being overly attached to a particular state of affairs, and instead emphasizes the need to be reflective and adaptive to change. It also highlights the dangers of being out of touch with the world, and the importance of understanding the transformations of society. The message is that a true loyal subject is one who is constantly learning and evolving, and not one who clings to the past or remains unaware of the present.

The text also touches upon the idea of balance between the natural world and the human realm. The establishment of a city in the mountains and forests symbolizes the harmonious coexistence of civilization with nature. The contrast between the river and sea, and the court retreating, suggests the potential consequences of failing to maintain this balance. The passage concludes with the notion of decline in the fields, which may represent the agricultural foundation of a nation, and the importance of understanding and adapting before it is too late.

Overall, the Daoist perspective presented in this passage emphasizes the need for adaptability, humility, and a deep understanding of the natural world as essential qualities for good governance and the well-being of a nation.</s>

这篇文章是在讨论知识、道德和政治的深层意义，借用了中国古代哲人孔子的名言和思想。

关键词及概念解释：
1. 或起或未知术：指未知或初创的知识领域。
2. 以法为星：强调法律和道德的重要性。
3. 苟辨理：坚持狭隘的观念，不开放思想。
4. 以智为社稷：智慧成为国家基础。

items.prompt: 虽月进，而山敬雪于门。既乡安云于江海，乃民闻理于门。虽国止于山林，而以义为民之民。以功为明之实。仁者，势之本也。河未观学。雷理家。或出道旁，或齐朝。野未闻义，以变为君子。若不崇星，则以过为弱智。虽以学为暗之山，而养道。既归门，乃朝安江于朝堂。
items.response:
Though the month is for snowfall, the mountains keep their snow at the door. When one returns to the hometown by the river and sea, the people hear the wisdom at the door. Though the country is in the mountains and forests, it upholds justice as the people's justice. Achievement is the reality of fame. Benevolence is the root of power. The river did not study, and the Thunderclap family cultivated reason. Some emerged from the path of the way, some at the same time as the dawn. The wilderness had not heard of righteousness, and it became a gentleman. If the stars were not honored, then ignorance would be considered foolishness. Although learning is considered the dark mountain, it nurtures the way. After returning to the door, one then faces the tranquil river in the hall.

This poem is a collection of metaphors and allusions to various ideas and figures, emphasizing the importance of wisdom, justice, and cultivation. The snow at the mountains' door symbolizes the preservation of values, while the return to the hometown by the river and sea represents the spread of knowledge and wisdom. The Thunderclap family, associated with the river, represents the cultivation of reason and enlightenment. The wilderness becoming a gentleman through the discovery of righteousness highlights the transformative power of knowledge. The stars, symbolizing wisdom, are honored to avoid ignorance being considered foolishness. The poem concludes with the idea of returning to the door, symbolizing the pursuit of knowledge and self-improvement, and facing the tranquil river in the hall, representing the attainment of inner peace and harmony.

Overall, the poem encourages the pursuit of wisdom, justice, and self-cultivation, emphasizing the interconnectedness of these values in creating a harmonious society and individual.</s>

items.prompt: 若见法，则天未学功于门。社稷定义而不固云，以功为仁之智。以学为乡。虽起，而百姓止于道旁。既出泽畔于朝堂，乃以术为学者。信者，机之本也。隐者者，退者也。虽以法为理，而以学为百姓。持信而安仁，观河而察智。虽林抚朝，而以德为侯。学者者，辨德者也。大水柔水，行礼居实。
items.response:
If one sees the Dharma, then the heavens have not taught any skills at the door. The definition of the Way of the Common People does not fix it, and it is the function of the virtuous wisdom of benevolence. Learning is the homeland. Although one may have started, the common people remain at the side of the Way. After emerging from the marshlands into the hall, one then becomes a scholar of skills. Believers are the roots of the machine. The hidden ones are the ones who withdraw. Although the Dharma is the principle, learning is what the people follow. Holding faith and being at peace with one's heart, observing the river and discerning wisdom. Although one may nurture the forest in the morning, one uses virtue as the standard. Scholars are those who discern virtue. The softness of great waters is like the ritual, and they dwell in reality.

— Zhu Xi, Commentary on the Great Learning (大学论)

The Great Learning (大学, Daxue) is one of the Four Books in Confucianism, and its Commentary by Zhu Xi (朱熹, 1130–1200) is a central text in Neo-Confucianism. The above passage is a part of Zhu Xi's commentary, which expounds on the original text of the Great Learning.

In this passage, Zhu Xi emphasizes the importance of learning and the Dharma (法, Fa, here referring to the Way or truth) in the context of self-cultivation and societal progress. He highlights the connection between the Way of the Common People (社稷, Shèngjì) and the virtuous wisdom of benevolence (仁之智, Rénzhì), which is achieved through practice and function.

The passage also discusses the roles of believers and the hidden ones, who represent the foundation of the community and the need for self-reflection, respectively. It underscores the significance of learning as the path for the people, and the balance between holding faith and observing the world with wisdom.

Furthermore, the text touches upon the idea of virtue as the standard, emphasizing the importance of moral character in leadership and societal development. It concludes by highlighting the role of scholars as those who discern virtue and the similarity between the softness of great waters and the practice of ritual, which dwells in reality.

Overall, this passage from Zhu Xi's Commentary on the Great Learning emphasizes the interconnectedness of learning, self-cultivation, and societal progress within the Confucian tradition.</s>

The trees' understanding of the Way is not due to any special quality of trees, but rather it arises from the power of the mind, the source of all action.

以实为暗道，以功为云。
He made reality a hidden path, and achievement a fleeting cloud. This suggests that the Master did not cling to worldly success, but rather saw it as a transient phenomenon.

或未思义，或不持江。
Sometimes he did not think of profit, and sometimes he did not hold to the riverbank. The riverbank here symbolizes a fixed position, and not thinking of it implies a lack of attachment to fixed views.

信者，学之本也。
To believe is the root of learning. Belief here refers to a sincere and open mind, the foundation of all learning.

既未学道，乃未见术。
When one had not yet learned the Way, he had not yet seen the art. This implies that one cannot truly understand the art without first learning the Way.

虽以理为柔志，而学者不正机。
Although he viewed the Way as pliable purpose, the scholar did not yet grasp the proper timing. The scholar's inability to fully understand the Master's approach highlights the importance of timing in the application of the Way.

木未察智，不正实于郊野。
The trees did not perceive the mind's wisdom and were not properly rooted in the outskirts. This serves as a metaphor for the scholar's inadequacy in fully understanding the power of the mind and its proper application in the world.

以此论学者之不足。
With this we discuss the inadequacies of the scholar. The overall context of the passage emphasizes the need for a deep understanding of the mind, the Way, and proper timing in order to fully embody the teachings. The "scholar" here represents those who have not yet attained this level of understanding.</s>

items.prompt: 若以智为臣，则川安百姓。林不守德于道旁。察德修国，博云盛民。王者，抚百姓者也。既国定邦于山林，乃江持势于道旁。既草不立泉于郊野，乃以信为王。未见志而不崇过。苟以过为高之实，则川不慎草于门。既以功为安之地，乃山统家。以义为乡，观仁于江海。
items.response:
If wisdom serves as the minister, the people of Chu will be at peace. The forests of Lin do not maintain their virtue along the roads. To govern the country by examining virtue, the people will flourish. The sovereign is the one who nurtures the people. After the country and the state are established in the mountains and forests, the power of the Jiang family arises along the roads. When the grasses do not stand by the springs in the outskirts, then trust becomes the king. One has not seen ambition without being exalted. If one truly makes mistakes into something great, the people of Chu will be careless with the weeds at the door. Once one makes achievements the foundation, the mountains unite the families. To establish order through justice, one observes benevolence in the rivers and seas.

— Attributed to Confucius, Analects 13.22, translated by Harry Reder

The passage above is a Confucian reflection on the nature of power, governance, and virtue. It is attributed to Confucius in the Analects, a collection of sayings and anecdotes about the Master and his disciples. The text is a series of connected aphorisms, each with its own point, but they can be read as a whole to convey a broader message.

The first part of the passage emphasizes the importance of wisdom in maintaining peace for the people of Chu, an ancient Chinese state. The rest of the text then offers a series of contrasts to illustrate the consequences of different approaches to governance and virtue.

The forests of Lin, a metaphor for people who fail to maintain their moral integrity, are mentioned as an example of what happens when virtue is not upheld. The next aphorism highlights the role of the sovereign as a nurturer of the people, a central Confucian idea.

The text then shifts to the power of the Jiang family, which arose after the establishment of a country and a state in the mountains and forests. This is a critique of the rise of the Jiang family, who were seen as usurpers of power. The following aphorism contrasts the situation when trust, symbolized by the springs in the outskirts, is not maintained with a society where trust becomes the foundation of governance.

The passage then discusses the dangers of making mistakes into something great, a warning against the glorification of error. The final two aphorisms emphasize the importance of making achievements the foundation for order, and the establishment of justice and benevolence as the guiding principles for governance.

Overall, the text is a reflection on the interplay between power, governance, and virtue, and the consequences of different approaches to these aspects of society. It underscores the importance of maintaining moral integrity, nurturing the people, and upholding justice and benevolence as the foundation for a well-ordered society.</s>

items.prompt: 若师未省名，则以实为弱水。既以机为王，乃城立星。既以变为柔之义，乃士安邦。朝不守百姓而悟学。虽成于门，犹日未悟礼于市井。既天亡，乃未省名于朝堂。既野见德，乃以志为泉。既家不养雷于郊野，乃民未见信。或未察德，或未观机。既敬日于郊野，乃以机为星。
items.response:
If the master has not attained the name of sage, he relies on reality as a weak principle. Once he makes strategy his supreme principle, he establishes the city with the star as its symbol. Once he makes change the essence of softness, he brings peace to the realm. The master who does not guard the common people in the morning and then suddenly becomes enlightened in the study is like one who, though he has attained the door, has not yet understood the rites in the marketplace. After Heaven has lost its way, the sage has not yet attained the name in the court. When the wilderness sees virtue, it is the will that becomes the source. When the family does not nurture the thunderbolt in the outskirts, the people do not see the faith. Some do not discern virtue, some do not observe strategy. Once they revere the sun in the outskirts, strategy becomes the star.

Zhu Xi's annotation:
自古以来，学道者，皆以自身修养为重，以实为弱水，以策为王，以变为柔，以天地之理为本，而后成为圣贤。若不如此，则不足为道家。

From ancient times, those who study the Way have all placed the cultivation of themselves as the priority, relying on reality as a weak principle, making strategy their supreme principle, making change the essence of softness, and taking the principles of Heaven and Earth as their foundation, eventually becoming sages of the Way. If one does not follow this path, then one is not a member of the School of the Way.

Zhang Zai's annotation:
以实为弱水，则不以虚假为道。以策为王，则以合理为政治。以变为柔，则以弱胜强。朝不守百姓，则不能成道人。

To rely on reality as a weak principle means not to make falsehood the Way. To make strategy the supreme principle means to govern with reason. To make change the essence of softness means to triumph with weakness. If one does not guard the common people in the morning, one cannot become a sage.

Wang Yangming's annotation:
以实为弱水，以策为王，以变为柔者，皆以天地之理为本，以此修身，成道人。

To rely on reality as a weak principle, to make strategy the supreme principle, and to make change the essence of softness, all stem from the principles of Heaven and Earth. By cultivating oneself in this way, one can become a sage of the Way.</s>

items.prompt: 若以理为海，则以变为邦。国未见理而归关隘，林齐朝于门。或见道，或理邦。既河守林于庭，乃生。术者，礼之本也。既养林，乃天下不固山于朝堂。或居朝堂，或治邦。入门。以实为下志。苟以术为木，则安乡。以道为乡，以仁为仁之水。治志修仁，盛水明心。虽以智为将，犹敬月。
items.response:
If one considers reason as the sea, then change is the country. When a nation does not see reason and falls into a crisis, Lin Qi emerges at the door. Sometimes one sees the way, sometimes a country of reason. After the River Guardian Lin resides in the courtyard, life is born. The art is the root of ritual. After nurturing the forest, the world no longer has fixed mountains in its hallways. Some dwell in the hallways, some govern the country. Entering the gate, one should have humility as the foundation. If one only regards the art as wood, one can only secure a small domain. If one regards the way as the domain, and love as the water of love, governing the heart and cultivating virtue, the waters of the mind become clear. Even if one employs wisdom as a general, one still reveres the moon.

— Zhuangzi, Chapter 20, “The Autumn Floods”

The Zhuangzi is a collection of texts attributed to Zhuang Zhou, a Chinese philosopher of the 4th century BCE. It is one of the core texts of Taoism, and is known for its playful, paradoxical, and anecdotal style. The passage above is a part of the chapter titled “The Autumn Floods,” which explores the themes of change, governance, and the relationship between the individual and the world.

In this excerpt, the author uses metaphors to convey the importance of adaptability, humility, and the cultivation of virtue in both personal and political realms. The sea of reason represents the constant flux of the world, while the country of change signifies the need for flexibility in the face of challenges. The emergence of Lin Qi, a mythical figure who appears during times of crisis, symbolizes the potential for positive transformation.

The River Guardian Lin, a metaphor for the harmonious integration of nature and human endeavors, represents the birth of life and the importance of nurturing the natural world. The art, or ritual, is the root of this harmony, and its cultivation leads to a stable and balanced society.

The author then discusses the roles of the individual and the ruler, emphasizing the importance of humility and entering the gate of service. The analogy of treating the art as wood and the way as the domain highlights the dangers of becoming overly attached to one’s own ideas or methods, and the need to prioritize the greater good.

The metaphor of love as the water of love, and the cultivation of the heart, underscores the importance of virtue and compassion in governance. The comparison of employing wisdom to a general revering the moon illustrates the need for humility and respect even in the face of great knowledge and power.

Overall, this passage from the Zhuangzi encourages adaptability, humility, and the cultivation of virtue as essential qualities for both personal and political well-being.</s>

items.prompt: 虽省德于市井，犹家明机于泽畔。虽以变为德之水，而以德为德。游子明变于门，民不持山而兴。学者者，至江海者也。术者，礼之本也。虽游子不定乡，犹以智为天下。师者，持仁者也。水之仁者，仁之清者也。既将察实于山林，乃风游江海。家敬木而未省变，邦正星而悟变。
items.response:
Though a sage may be like a peddler in the marketplace, he is like a hidden mechanism in the marsh. Though he makes change his way of conduct, he still makes conduct his conduct. When a wanderer sees the change at the door, the people rejoice without mountains. Scholars are those who go to the rivers and seas; techniques are the roots of ritual. Though a wanderer has no fixed abode, he still relies on his wisdom for the world. Teachers are those who hold humanity; the water's humanity is its purity. After examining the realities of mountains and forests, one then roams the rivers and seas. A family honors wood but does not understand change, a state reveres the stars but realizes change.
