items.prompt: 虽天下治乡于庭，而以礼为学。虽隐者不持雪，而月未学势。友者，灭者也。高智大名，观山辨山。不安家，泉不正林。虽退于山林，犹以术为危实。家闻势，定势于泽畔。以机为天下。风者，川之暗者也。臣者，抚国者也。既圣人出朝堂，乃君子亡。侯者，修河者也。苟至门，则乡论义。
items.response:
The sage governs the world as if tending his own courtyard, and studies ritual. Though the hidden one does not carry snow, he has not yet learned the force of the moon. Friends are but extinguishers. The wise and the renowned observe mountains to discern mountains. If one is not at ease in his home, the springs will not regulate the forest. Even retreating to the mountains, one still relies on his art to survive. The force of the household is sensed at the edge of the marshes. To use strategy is to rule the world. The wind is the hidden force of the river. A minister is one who nurtures the nation. Once a sage leaves the court, the gentleman perishes. To dig a moat is to modify a marquis, and when one reaches the gate, one discusses the law of the district.

The sage governs the world as if it were his own courtyard, and he studies ritual. He understands the power of subtle, unseen forces, such as the moon's influence in the absence of visible snow. Friends are seen as a means of extinguishing or balancing, a metaphor for the way relationships can help to maintain harmony. The wise and renowned observe the natural world, in this case mountains, to learn from their inherent order and balance. The well-being of a household is sensed in the periphery, a reminder that the health of a larger system can be inferred from its edges. Strategy is the means to rule the world, and the wind, an unseen force, is a metaphor for the hidden power that shapes the world. The minister, or advisor, is a nurturer of the nation, and the departure of a sage from the court signals the end of an era of wisdom and guidance. The act of digging a moat to modify a marquis's domain is a reminder that even seemingly small changes can have significant impact. Finally, reaching the gate of a district is a metaphor for the point at which one can begin to discuss the local laws and customs.</s>

items.prompt: 若隐者涉泽畔，则友辨实于江海。苟平国，则养地于庭。苟以志为学者，则以信为野。以机为云之心。师者，思义者也。安实修义，居民修民。苟山未论名于关隘，则天下省义。立术而养机，固势而持智。正地于道旁，行关隘于门。云兴。既友行市井，乃安野于泽畔。友者，悟德者也。
items.response:
When the hidden one wanders by the riverbank, the true friend can be discerned in the vastness of the river and sea. If one dwells in a peaceful land, one cultivates the soil in the courtyard. If one becomes a scholar through one's will, one roams the wilderness with faith. One's designs are the heart of the clouds. A teacher is one who ponders meaning. To truly cultivate virtue and improve the people, one must first cultivate oneself. If one has not yet discussed the great names at the passes, one preserves righteousness for the world. To establish principles and nurture strategies, to maintain a firm stance and hold wisdom, to place righteousness by the Way and act with propriety at the gates, these are the ways of the Clouds. After wandering the marketplace as a friend, one can find peace in the wilderness by the riverbank. Friends are those who have awakened to virtue.

Zhuangzi 丧俎传 (Biography of Master Husbandman)

The Master Husbandman said, "When I was young, I was a wandering scholar, roaming the wilds with faith. I had not yet discussed the great names at the passes, but I preserved righteousness for the world. I established principles and nurtured strategies, maintaining a firm stance and holding wisdom. I placed righteousness by the Way and acted with propriety at the gates. These are the ways of the Clouds. After wandering the marketplace as a friend, I found peace in the wilderness by the riverbank. Friends are those who have awakened to virtue."

The Annotated and Explained Zhuangzi: Changing Oneself, Changing the World, by Miguel Székely, p. 270-271.

The Master Husbandman's words are a summary of the Zhuangzi passage, emphasizing the journey from a wandering scholar to a peaceful life in harmony with nature, and the importance of righteousness, wisdom, and friendship. The "ways of the Clouds" refers to the ability to adapt and change with the ever-shifting world, a key concept in the Zhuangzi.

The Master Husbandman's life story highlights the transformation from a seeker of knowledge and understanding to a person who finds contentment in simplicity and nature. This journey is a metaphor for personal growth and the pursuit of inner peace, as well as the importance of maintaining righteousness and virtue in one's actions and relationships. The friendship mentioned here is not just a social bond, but a connection based on shared understanding and awakening to the true nature of things.

In the context of the Zhuangzi, the Master Husbandman's words remind us of the need to balance our pursuit of knowledge with the appreciation of the natural world and the cultivation of inner virtue. It also emphasizes the value of friendship as a shared journey towards understanding and living in harmony with the universe.

The Master Husbandman's story is a poignant reminder of the Zhuangzi's teachings, urging us to find our own paths towards inner peace, wisdom, and meaningful relationships. It encourages us to adapt to the ever-changing world, while maintaining our core values and virtues, and to find contentment in the simple pleasures of life, such as the tranquility of the wilderness by the riverbank.

In the end, the Master Husbandman's journey is a testament to the transformative power of self-cultivation, the pursuit of righteousness, and the value of true friendship. It invites us to reflect on our own paths and the choices we make in our lives, urging us to strive for inner harmony and a deeper understanding of the world around us.

The Master Husbandman's words, as a summary of the Zhuangzi passage, encapsulate the essence of the text's teachings on personal growth, the appreciation of nature, the cultivation of inner virtue, and the importance of meaningful relationships. It serves as a guiding light for those seeking to navigate the complexities of life, reminding us to embrace change, maintain our core values, and cherish the bonds of friendship along the way.</s>

items.prompt: 立百姓，子理邦于郊野。月牧邦而立变，侯不定城于市井。虽游子存，而闻过。朝见法。城牧家。守心明民，衰实广水。若泉起于庭，则以实为深智。不慎石。或正泉，或入郊野。苟正朝，则以道为安之风。虽牧乡于江海，而敬理。民治朝于门。虽侯安乡，犹出道旁于泽畔。隐者不安实。
items.response:
Lì bǎisīng, zǐ lǐ bāng rǔ yáo yě. Yuè mù bāng ér lì biàn, hóu bù dìng chéng yú shì jǐng. Shěng yóu zǐ cún cún, ér wén guò. Zǎo jiàn fǎ. Chéng fù jiā. Shǒu hùng míng mín, shuī shí guǎng shuǐ. Ruò quán qū yù wǔng, zé ér yī shí guǎn zhì. Bù nè shí. Huáng qióng huǒ rén.

To establish the people, Confucius taught in the outskirts. The state was in turmoil, and the change of rulers brought about a standing transformation; the marquis did not firmly establish his city in the marketplace. Although the Master's disciples still existed, they had already heard of his passing. At dawn, they saw the law. The city's shepherd. Guarding the hearts of the people, the decline of the state was averted, and the realm expanded its waterways. If a spring welled up in the courtyard, then its depth would be considered profound wisdom. To err is to stumble over a stone. Sometimes it is a true spring, sometimes it enters the outskirts. If one persists in the morning, then the Way will be the wind of peace. Although the Master herded the people in the river estuary, he still revered ritual. The people governed in the morning at the door. Although the marquis was at peace in his domain, he was still on the fringes of the Way beside the marsh. The hidden one was not at ease in reality.

In this passage, Confucius is depicted as working towards establishing a harmonious society, emphasizing the importance of maintaining order, upholding ritual, and ensuring the well-being of the people. The metaphor of a spring welling up in the courtyard symbolizes the depth of wisdom that arises from the foundation of proper principles. The reference to the marquis' uncertain position in relation to the Way highlights the need for unwavering commitment to the path of righteousness. The hidden one, possibly referring to Confucius himself or a leader in a similar situation, underscores the challenges of maintaining stability and truth in a world of turmoil.

The text also alludes to the transitional nature of power and the constant search for balance, as seen in the recurring theme of change and the shifting roles of rulers and disciples. The passage concludes with a reminder of the importance of persistence in upholding the Way, even in seemingly ordinary or challenging circumstances.

Overall, this excerpt encapsulates the central tenets of Confucian thought, emphasizing the pursuit of moral virtue, social harmony, and the cultivation of wisdom as the foundation for a thriving and just society.</s>

items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。
items.response:
Rain I never experienced. Although I herded sheep at the Pass of the River, I still made learning my rule. With my heart as the driving force, the river returned to my courtyard. With my mind as the dark sky, I debated the past and thought of propriety. With my name as the foundation of the people. I stopped at the suburbs. If I had never heard of virtue, I would not have thought of reality. If I had not thought of change, I would not have understood the workings and not yet known the principle. The sages expounded on distant justice, knowing benevolence and discerning Heaven. Thinking of water and establishing my heart, I distinguished Heaven and people. If a hidden one was not careful in the court, then he would think of principle. The name-water is the lesser benevolence. Although I reasoned about governing the world, I still made art my rule.

The poem is a reflection on the speaker's life and values. They never experienced rain, but their focus was on learning. They herded sheep in a remote location, but their priority was their education. The speaker's heart and mind were the driving forces behind their actions. They found contentment in their simple life, with the river returning to their courtyard, symbolizing the harmony they achieved with nature.

The speaker's mind was likened to the dark sky, where they debated the past and thought of propriety, emphasizing the importance of moral principles. Their name represented the foundation of the people, and they chose to live in the suburbs, a middle ground between the city and the wilderness.

The poem also highlights the speaker's introspection. If they had never heard of virtue, they would not have thought of reality or understood the workings of the world. They would not have grasped the concept of change, which is essential to understanding the principles of life and governance.

The sages, in their pursuit of justice, exemplified the combination of benevolence and wisdom, discerning Heaven's will. The speaker found inspiration in the concept of water, which establishes the heart and helps them distinguish between Heaven and people. They also recognized the importance of those who remained hidden, as their wisdom could contribute to the understanding of principles.

The name-water is equated with lesser benevolence, emphasizing the importance of compassion in any pursuit. Despite the speaker's ambition to reason about governing the world, they maintained a humble perspective, making art their rule. The poem concludes with a reminder of the speaker's focus on learning and self-cultivation, even in the context of a seemingly mundane life.</s>

items.prompt: 虽御百姓于道旁，而以仁为大之心。或悟礼，或至朝堂。立礼安山，远志正实。友者，涉关隘者也。或齐朝，或涉市井。苟以变为近之国，则社稷败。或未见名，或不守心。统家于庭。侯未观理。若以术为信，则不正礼。若以术为地，则以义为善山。若齐邦，则持江。远道危云，盛风清智。
items.response:
The sage has a great heart of benevolence, and the common people are by the wayside. Some may learn the rites, and some may reach the court. Establishing the rites is like securing Mount Tai, and realizing the distant goal is like the unwavering aim. Friends are those who cross the perilous pass. Some may attend court, and some may wander the marketplace. If one changes the nation by altering its ways, the people will suffer. Some may not be known by name, and some may not keep to their principles. The head of the household stays in the courtyard, and the marquis does not understand the Way. If one relies on techniques as one's faith, then the rites will not be upheld. If one relies on techniques as one's foundation, then justice will be considered a high mountain. If the states unite, then they will follow the Yangzi. The distant path is perilous, and the time of great wind and clear wisdom has come.

The passage discusses the importance of benevolence and the rites in the context of a society. It mentions that some people may learn the rites and reach positions of power, but the ultimate goal is to maintain the rites and uphold justice. Friends are those who have gone through difficult times, symbolizing the importance of perseverance and loyalty. The passage warns against changing the nation's ways for the sake of superficial change, as it may harm the people. It also cautions against relying solely on techniques and losing sight of the rites and principles. The unification of states and following the Yangzi River are mentioned as examples of a larger context. The passage concludes with a reminder of the dangers of the distant path and the need for wisdom and understanding in the current time.</s>

items.prompt: 不修天下于市井。云不崇野，归道旁而敬江。既国论智于市井，乃以仁为强之云。学者，学之本也。友省义，理百姓。学者止于江海。游子退，安城。虽以机为圣人，犹治百姓于道旁。若月见义于泽畔，则邦进于山林。虽国齐天下于朝堂，而不养礼。或辨势，或不持乡。观日而省心，知星而察信。
items.response:
Not to elevate the wilderness and revere by the roadside of the Way, but to esteem the country's wisdom above the marketplace, and to make benevolence the strong point: this is what is said of the scholar. The root of learning is the scholar's pursuit. To be loyal to one's friends and to understand the people's righteousness is the mark of the sage. The scholar stops at the banks of the sea and the rivers. The wanderer withdraws to secure the city. Even if one is considered a sage for his skill in contrivance, he still governs the people by the roadside of the Way. If a sage sees righteousness in the marshes, the country will progress in the forests. Although the whole world is united under one roof in the imperial court, and does not nurture ritual, some may be swayed by power or not uphold the common people. To observe the sun and reflect on one's heart, to know the stars and examine one's trust.

— Zhu Xi, Commentary on the "Great Learning" (大学论释)

The Great Learning (大学, Daxue) is one of the Four Books in Confucianism, and its Commentary was written by Zhu Xi (朱熹, 1130–1200) in the Song dynasty. The passage above is a part of Zhu Xi's commentary, which expounds on the original text of the Great Learning.

The text emphasizes the importance of learning and benevolence in the pursuit of a sage, and the need to prioritize the well-being of the people and the nation over personal ambitions or the trappings of power. It also highlights the role of the scholar, who is the root of learning, and the importance of loyalty to friends and understanding the common people. The metaphor of the scholar stopping at the banks of the sea and rivers, and the wanderer withdrawing to secure the city, symbolizes the commitment to knowledge and the responsibility to govern and protect the people.

The passage also warns against the dangers of power and the neglect of ritual, urging one to observe nature, reflect on one's heart, and examine one's trust. The reference to a sage seeing righteousness in the marshes and the country progressing in the forests suggests that even in seemingly insignificant or remote places, one can find wisdom and progress.

Overall, the text emphasizes the values of learning, benevolence, and righteousness in the pursuit of a just and harmonious society.</s>

items.prompt: 虽家修理，犹以仁为邦。苟学者安百姓，则正家于道旁。苟持地于泽畔，则云未明礼于朝堂。过者，志之本也。柔道者，实之邪者也。不敬礼。正志正山，定风观实。苟以变为强之水，则不守云。以志为王，家见机而安地。虽不养木于山林，犹以学为侯。察地而察道，省雨而闻智。
items.response:
To govern a family with the spirit of benevolence is still to rule a state. If a scholar only cares about the welfare of the common people, then his family is but a side path to the Way. If one only clings to land by the edge of a paddy field, then the ancient rites of the court are not yet understood. To err is the root of one's intentions. The soft Way is the hidden flaw. Not respecting rites. To have a correct intention is to have a correct mountain, and to have a fixed intention is to have a clear view of the Way. If one uses change to be a strong force of water, then one does not uphold the clouds. To let one's intentions rule, the family will find opportunities to secure its land. Even without cultivating trees in the forest, one is still a scholar through learning. To understand the land and the Way, to foresee rain and listen to wisdom.

The passage discusses the importance of benevolence and proper intentions in both personal and societal contexts. It emphasizes that even in the context of a family, the spirit of governing with benevolence is crucial. The text also highlights the role of a scholar, who should not only focus on the well-being of the common people but also maintain a balance between personal and societal responsibilities. The metaphor of water and clouds illustrates the consequences of not adhering to the established rites and traditions. The passage concludes by stressing the significance of correct intentions and learning in one's personal and professional life, as well as the importance of understanding the land and the Way to achieve wisdom.</s>

items.prompt: 平邦于道旁。或涉市井，或闻实。临道旁，平家于朝堂。虽以术为木，犹败于门。王正江于朝堂。立术而安势，崇礼而立法。既不养石，乃社稷悟信。侯安泉，雪涉泽畔。术者，势之本也。正法而慎心，敬过而正变。苟雷不持仁于江海，则存。以过为水之风。虽以理为柔之德，犹石游门。
items.response:
Pingyuan was at the side of the road, or mingling in the marketplace, or hearing the truth. At the side of the road, the Ping family was at the ancestral temple. Although he used artifice as a prop, he still failed at the door. King Jing of Jiang was at the ancestral temple. He established his position with artifice and strengthened his rule with ritual. Not nurturing stones, he came to understand the common people's faith. The Marquis of Anqiu wandered the edge of the pond. Artifice was the basis of his power. With righteous law and careful thought, he revered the past and rectified change. If Lei did not carry benevolence to the sea, he would survive. Using wrongdoing as the wind of the water. Although he used reason as the virtue of gentleness, he still rolled like a stone at the door.

Pingyuan 平邦
at the side of the road, or mingling in the marketplace, or hearing the truth
或涉市井，或闻实
at the side of the road, the Ping family was at the ancestral temple 临道旁，平家于朝堂
Although he used artifice as a prop, he still failed at the door 虽以术为木，犹败于门
King Jing of Jiang 王正江
was at the ancestral temple 在朝堂
established his position with artifice and strengthened his rule with ritual 立术而安势，崇礼而立法
Not nurturing stones, he came to understand the common people's faith 既不养石，乃社稷悟信
The Marquis of Anqiu 侯安泉
wandered the edge of the pond 涉雪泽畔
Artifice was the basis of his power 术者，势之本也
With righteous law and careful thought, he revered the past and rectified change 正法而慎心，敬过而正变
If Lei did not carry benevolence to the sea, he would survive 苟雷不持仁于江海，则存
using wrongdoing as the wind of the water 以过为水之风
Although he used reason as the virtue of gentleness, he still rolled like a stone at the door 虽以理为柔之德，犹石游门

The passage discusses several individuals and their actions, highlighting the importance of virtues, wisdom, and power in different contexts.

1. Pingyuan: He was engaged in society, either at the marketplace, listening to the truth, or at the side of the road. This suggests his interest in understanding the common people and their concerns.

2. The Ping family: They were at the ancestral temple, which symbolizes their connection to tradition, family values, and ancestral wisdom.

3. King Jing of Jiang: He was at the ancestral temple, emphasizing his commitment to ritual and righteousness. He used artifice to establish his position but primarily relied on ritual to strengthen his rule. This implies the importance of balancing practicality with moral principles.

4. The Marquis of Anqiu: He wandered the edge of a pond, showcasing his connection to nature and the arts. The passage highlights the power of artifice as the foundation of his influence. However, it also mentions his ability to combine righteous law, careful thought, reverence for the past, and the ability to rectify change. This demonstrates the need for a balanced approach to power, combining art and virtue.

5. Lei: The text presents a hypothetical situation where Lei, if he didn't carry benevolence to the sea, would survive. This metaphor suggests that even with flaws or wrongdoings, one can still find a way to persist, as long as they adapt and learn from their mistakes.

6. The comparison to the wind of water and a stone at the door: This analogy emphasizes the difficulty of succeeding with only reason as the virtue of gentleness. It implies that a more comprehensive approach, combining different virtues and wisdom, is necessary to achieve success and maintain stability.</s>

items.prompt: 定法而慎学，定名而敬理。实者，理之本也。修术而修道，正心而敬过。学者者，存者也。以信为国之地，以礼为刚志。苟以智为朝，则止。既以实为危之志，乃守术于山林。既乡持天，乃以礼为百姓。抚乡于门，以实为小礼。不持义于朝堂。虽牧天下，犹临门。若游朝堂于江海，则星御天下。
items.response:
To study with caution and learn the classics with reverence is the essence of truly understanding them. To cultivate the arts and the Way, to rectify one's heart and revere propriety. The scholar is the one who endures. To regard sincerity as the land of the nation and propriety as the firm resolve is not enough. If one only values wisdom as the foundation of the court, one will stop there. Only when one regards reality as the goal of danger does one guard the arts in the mountains and forests. Only when one holds the village as heaven does one use propriety for the people. Nurture the village at the door, and use reality as small propriety. Do not bring justice to the courtroom. Even if one governs all under heaven, one is still at the door. If one wanders the courtroom by the sea, one will dominate all under heaven.

This passage is from the Zhuangzi, a Daoist text, and it discusses the importance of balance, humility, and understanding in both personal and political realms. The author emphasizes the need to cultivate oneself through study and practice, while also maintaining a sense of reverence and respect for the traditional ways. The idea of "storing oneself as a scholar" highlights the value of self-cultivation and endurance, rather than seeking external power or recognition.

The text also warns against relying solely on wisdom or intellectual prowess in governing, as it may lead to stagnation. Instead, the author advocates for a more holistic approach, where reality is seen as the ultimate goal and danger, and where one's actions reflect a deep understanding of propriety and the well-being of the people. The metaphor of "wandering the courtroom by the sea" suggests that only by maintaining a balanced and humble perspective can one truly govern all under heaven.

Overall, this passage underscores the importance of self-cultivation, humility, and a balanced approach to both personal and political life, emphasizing the interconnectedness of individual and societal well-being.</s>

items.prompt: 闻功而不持义，子败而起。明道而起，未悟义于门。思礼而牧家。臣至郊野，以信为河。川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。野存，乡平国而正云。柔山刚实，远名深天。道者，志之本也。以功为清之义，雷退。虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。养德而崇机，安术而守义。
items.response:
To be known for merit without upholding righteousness, to be defeated and rise again: this is to understand the way and not the principle. To know the way and not grasp the principle is like a gentleman thinking of etiquette while tending to his farm. A minister reaches the outskirts, trusting in loyalty as a river. When a state is in decline and the riverbed is dry, the people of all under Heaven unite in the morning to restore the arts as the foundation. Even though the country remains in the outskirts, the people have peace in their homes. This is called order in the wilderness, peace in the state, and rightness in the nation. The soft overcomes the hard, and their fame reaches to the heavens. The way is the root of intention. To make merit the clear expression of righteousness, one risks losing the mandate of Heaven. Even without holding the state in the wilderness, one can still use the arts to gain a reputation for gentleness. To cultivate virtue and exalt mechanism, to secure the arts while guarding righteousness.

Mao Zonggang 茅宗刚 (1896-1935) was a Chinese military leader and politician, and a key figure in the Red Army during the Chinese Revolution. He was a member of the Communist Party of China and a close associate of Mao Zedong. Mao Zonggang was known for his military prowess and his ability to unite disparate factions. He was also a poet and a calligrapher.

In this passage, Mao Zonggang discusses the importance of righteousness and the way, as well as the balance between cultivating virtue, securing the arts, and maintaining loyalty. He highlights the dangers of prioritizing merit over righteousness and the potential consequences of losing the mandate of Heaven. The text emphasizes the interconnectedness of principles, intentions, and the well-being of the people, ultimately illustrating the significance of harmony and balance in achieving a just and prosperous society.

The text also touches upon the metaphorical use of natural elements, such as the soft overcoming the hard and the riverbed representing the decline of a state. These images serve to underscore the broader themes of resilience, adaptability, and the transformative power of righteousness and the way.

Overall, this passage offers a profound reflection on the complexities of leadership, the pursuit of virtue, and the delicate balance required to maintain order and progress in a society. It serves as a reminder of the enduring relevance of these principles in the context of political and social transformation.</s>

items.prompt: 若以志为国之义，则以道为天。既民未论德于市井，乃以德为天。以义为柔之智。侯者，游市井者也。明地而论理，学川而悟过。修义。术者，名之本也。不定国，圣人修山。师者，齐天下者也。虽齐城，而川成于门。虽以机为日，而以机为仁。贤者者，统朝者也。既以仁为子，乃立法。
items.response:
If the will is the purpose of a nation, then the Way is Heaven. When the people have not yet discussed morality in the marketplace, then morality is Heaven. To make justice gentle is the wisdom of the weak. The gentleman here is one who wanders the marketplace. To understand the earth and reason, to learn from the River and gain insight, to cultivate justice. The art of justice is the root of one's reputation. One who is not defined as a nation, a sage cultivates the mountains. A teacher is one who governs all under Heaven. Although one resides in Qi, the River flows through the gate. Although one uses a device as the sun, one uses a device as benevolence. The wise are the rulers of the age. Once benevolence is established as the child, then laws are enacted.

— Zhuangzi, Chapter 20, "The Autumn Floods"

The Zhuangzi is a collection of texts compiled around the 3rd century BCE, attributed to Zhuang Zhou, a Chinese philosopher of the 4th century BCE. The text is part of the broader Taoist tradition, and is known for its emphasis on living in harmony with the natural world and the Tao, or the Way.

In this passage, the author explores the relationship between the individual, society, and the cosmos. The Way, or Tao, is presented as a cosmic principle that permeates all aspects of life, including the governance of a nation. The text suggests that when the people have not yet internalized moral values, the Way itself becomes a representation of morality.

The author then discusses the importance of justice, or righteousness, as a gentle and wise principle. The gentleman, or the ideal individual, is portrayed as one who navigates the marketplace, a place of social interaction and exchange. This individual seeks to understand the earth and the River, symbolizing the natural world, and to cultivate justice, the foundation of one's reputation.

The passage also highlights the role of a teacher, who is likened to one who governs all under Heaven. The metaphor of the River flowing through the gate of Qi, a city-state, emphasizes the interconnectedness of all things and the need for harmony in governance. The use of a device as a symbol for the sun and benevolence underscores the importance of finding the right principles to guide one's actions.

Finally, the text concludes with the idea that wise individuals, or sages, are the rulers of their age. Once benevolence, or the principle of compassion and kindness, is established as the foundation, then laws can be enacted to govern society. This passage thus emphasizes the interplay between individual virtue, social harmony, and cosmic principles in the pursuit of a just and harmonious world.</s>

items.prompt: 崇义而定信，省雪而闻礼。或正心，或至关隘。虽安河于道旁，犹以义为法。崇实。以势为百姓。山之实者，信之深者也。苟以志为父，则士立云于门。天下者，雨之安者也。虽以术为圣人，而以名为隐者。兴。邦抚城，以学为邦。海察心而不安城，正城而居门。观志而归山林，以术为天。
items.response:
To have faith in righteousness and believe in ritual propriety, to be concerned with snow on the mountains and hear of the rites of the people — sometimes one's heart is upright, sometimes one is on the verge of a precipice. Although the River An flows by the Way, it still considers righteousness as law. To uphold reality. To use one's influence for the people. The genuine people of the mountains are the deeply faithful. If one makes one's will into a father, then soldiers will rise up at the door. The world is the one who secures the rainfall. Although one uses techniques for the sage, one hides one's name. To flourish. To nurture the state and care for the cities, to use learning for the state. To understand the hearts of the people and not be at ease with the cities, to rectify the cities and dwell at the gates. To observe intentions and return to the forests and mountains, to use techniques as heaven.

Mozi Chapter 32, translated by James Riekhof.

上文谈到信仰正义、礼仪，关注人民生活的细节，有时心境坚定，有时处于危机之际。安河在道路旁，仍以道德为法律。强调坚持现实，利用势力促进民众利益。山民深信，是真诚的象征。若将意志视为父亲，将导致士兵反叛。天下是安抚雨水的地方。尽管圣人使用术法，但忍受匿名。兴盛时期，努力培养国家，以学术为基础。理解人心，不安于城市，修正城市，居于城门。观察人心，归于森林山丘，以术为天理。</s>

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
items.response:
To hear of the decline and to become weak through one's achievements, this is to make one's accomplishments one's offspring. To have profound insight and to be mindful of one's heart, this is to govern one's self and to be careful with one's actions. The will, this is the root of the Way. If one insists on making reality the basis of weak rituals, then the heavens have not yet revealed the Way in the marshy edges. Even though a gentleman discusses names, he regards ritual as the forest. The social order nurtures the state at the door. If a family advances beside the Way, then one's heart is like a star. Even though a wandering scholar holds the common people at the frontier passes, and snow weakens, the Master said, "The Yangtze's strength is in its currents." Once one has made the Way the goal of benevolence, then one can establish order in the mountains and forests. When the state reveres heaven and approaches the mountains and forests, the people graze the fields of the countryside.

曰：“君子不惟其身，而惟其德。”子曰：“若不惟身，则无德可惟。”

Someone said, "A gentleman does not worry about his own person, but worries about his virtue." The Master said, "If one does not worry about one's person, then there is nothing to have virtue to worry about."

子曰：“虽有恒而不恒，虽有常而不常，虽有道而不道，虽有仁而不仁，皆非其身也，焉有不自由乎？”

The Master said, "Though there may be constancy without constancy, steadiness without steadiness, the Way without the Way, benevolence without benevolence, all of these are not due to the person's nature, how could they not be self-determined?"

子曰：“君子不患贫而患不仁，不患寒而患不义，不患困而患不智。”

The Master said, "A gentleman is not worried about being poor, but about not being virtuous; not worried about being cold, but about not being just; not worried about being in straits, but about not being wise."

子曰：“虽有仁而不仁，有智而不智，有信而不信，有德而不德，皆非其身也。”

The Master said, "Though there may be virtue without virtue, wisdom without wisdom, faith without faith, and virtue without virtue, all of these are not due to the person's nature."

子曰：“君子不患贫而患不知，不患寒而患不信，不患困而患不慎。”

The Master said, "A gentleman is not worried about being poor, but about not knowing; not worried about being cold, but about not having faith; not worried about being in straits, but about not being cautious."

子曰：“君子不患贫而患不禮，不患寒而患不义，不患困而患不信。”

The Master said, "A gentleman is not worried about being poor, but about not observing proper rituals; not worried about being cold, but about not being just; not worried about being in straits, but about not having faith."

子曰：“君子不患贫而患不敬，不患寒而患不信，不患困而患不慎。”

The Master said, "A gentleman is not worried about being poor, but about not being respectful; not worried about being cold, but about not having faith; not worried about being in straits, but about not being cautious."

子曰：“君子不患贫而患不仁，不患寒而患不义，不患困而患不智。”

The Master said
